coredata.dc:title: Investigation of layered double hydroxide/carbon dot nanocomposite on removal efficiency of Pb2+ from aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Vasheghani Farahani, Fataneh
Amini, Mohammad Hassan
Ahmadi, Seyed Hamid
Zakaria, Seyed Amirabbas
coredata.dc:description: In this study, double layered hydroxide (LDH)/carbon dot (C-dot) nanocomposite was synthesized using coprecipitation and hydrothermal methods, respectively, and used to remove Pb2+ ions from water. The chemical composition and morphology of the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite structure were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), x-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FS). Batch adsorption was accomplished to evaluate the process of Pb2+ adsorption by Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite. For this purpose, parameters were optimized such as pH, C-dot to Mg-Al LDH ratio, adsorbent dosage, and contact time. The value obtained are 7, 0.25, 16 mg and 40 min, respectively. The results showed that the adsorption of Pb2+ on the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite has a maximum adsorption capacity of 41.32 (mg.g−1), which can be attributed to the available presence of functional groups. The adsorbent performance was investigated by the Langmuir, Freundlich and Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherms and the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, respectively. Adsorption isotherm followed the Langmuir equation, that demonstrate monolayer adsorption and the pseudo-second-order kinetic model shows that a chemical adsorption controls the reaction rate. According to the results, the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite can be considered as an efficient adsorbent for the adsorption of Pb2+ ion from aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Layered Double Hydroxide (LDH)
Adsorption
Pb2+ (Lead)
Carbon dot (C-dot)
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of C-dot to Mg-Al LDH ratio
Comparison of Pb2+ ion adsorption capacities with different adsorbents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support from Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of (a) Mg-Al LDH, (b) Mg-Al LDH/CD1, (c) Mg-Al LDH/CD2 and (d) Mg-Al LDH/CD3.
XRD pattern of (a) carbon dot, (b) Mg-Al LDH and (c) Mg-Al LDH/CD3.
SEM images of (a) Mg-Al LDH, (b) Mg-Al LDH/CD3 and (a-1 and b-1) larger scale images (a) and (b), respectively.
EDS spectrum of (a) Mg-Al-NO3 LDH and (b) Mg-Al LDH/CD3.
Normalized Fluorescence spectra (FS) of (a) CD1, (b) CD2 and (c) CD3.
Optimization of adsorption parameters. Effect of (a)pH, (b) C-dot to Mg-Al LDH ratio, (c) adsorbent dosage, (d) contact time on the removal of Pb2+ ion.
Effect of ionic strength (pH = 7, C0 = 10 mg.L-1, time = 90 min, CD/LDH = 0.25, volume = 40 mL and dosage = 0.25 mg).

(a) Comparison of heavy metal removal percentage by Mg-Al LDH/C-dots and LDH (b) Effect of interfering ion on removal Pb2+ with Mg-Al LDH/CD3 (pH = 7, C0 = 10 mg.L-1, time = 90 min, CD/LDH = 0.25, volume = 40 mL and dosage = 0.25 mg).

The Langmuir isotherm (a), Freundlich isotherm (b) and Dubinin-Radushkevich (D-R) isotherm (c) of Pb2+ adsorption onto Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite (pH = 7, C0 = 10 mg.L-1, time = 90 min, CD/LDH = 0.25, volume = 40 mL and dosage = 0.25 mg).

(a) the linear fit pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic equation of Pb2+ adsorption onto Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite, respectively, and (b) intra-particle diffusion rate constant (pH = 7, C0 = 10 mg.L-1, time = 90 min, CD/LDH = 0.25, volume = 40 mL and dosage = 0.25 mg).
Constants of adsorption isotherm parameters of Pb2+ onto Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite.
Constants of adsorption kinetic parameters of Pb2+ onto Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite.
Comparison of Pb2+ ion adsorption capacities with different adsorbents.

time = 120 min, T = 298 K
Graphene oxide based inverse spinel nickel ferrite (GONF)

time = 40 min, T = 298 K

head.ce:title: Investigation of layered double hydroxide/carbon dot nanocomposite on removal efficiency of Pb2+ from aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry& Chemical Engineering Reasearch Center Of Iran, Faculty of Environmental Analytical Chemistry and Clean Technologies, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry& Chemical Engineering Reasearch Center Of Iran
Faculty of Environmental Analytical Chemistry and Clean Technologies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry& Chemical Engineering Reasearch Center Of Iran, Faculty of Environmental Analytical Chemistry and Clean Technologies, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, double layered hydroxide (LDH)/carbon dot (C-dot) nanocomposite was synthesized using coprecipitation and hydrothermal methods, respectively, and used to remove Pb2+ ions from water. The chemical composition and morphology of the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite structure were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), x-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FS). Batch adsorption was accomplished to evaluate the process of Pb2+ adsorption by Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite. For this purpose, parameters were optimized such as pH, C-dot to Mg-Al LDH ratio, adsorbent dosage, and contact time. The value obtained are 7, 0.25, 16 mg and 40 min, respectively. The results showed that the adsorption of Pb2+ on the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite has a maximum adsorption capacity of 41.32 (mg.g−1), which can be attributed to the available presence of functional groups. The adsorbent performance was investigated by the Langmuir, Freundlich and Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherms and the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, respectively. Adsorption isotherm followed the Langmuir equation, that demonstrate monolayer adsorption and the pseudo-second-order kinetic model shows that a chemical adsorption controls the reaction rate. According to the results, the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite can be considered as an efficient adsorbent for the adsorption of Pb2+ ion from aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial growth and its inevitable consequences will lead to the production of pollutants that affect human health and the environment [1–3]. The surface waters are highly vulnerable to the entry of environmental, biological, chemical, organic, and heavy metal pollutants [4–7]. Unlike organic pollutants, heavy metals are non-degradable and dangerous, and because of their accumulation in the body, they cause a variety of diseases. Heavy metals in water include lead, mercury, cadmium, copper, nickel, tin and etc. [1]. The limit of Pb2+ in drinking water has been announced by various environmental organizations such as European Union (EU) and World Health Organization (WHO) equal to 0.010 mg of Pb2+ per liter [5]. For example, severe exposure to Pb2+ can cause brain dysfunction, nausea, vomiting, and chronic cases can cause anemia, encephalopathy, nephropathy, paralysis, and more [8]. Therefore, extensive research has been conducted on heavy metal removal [7–15]. Various techniques have been used to remove heavy metal ions, including precipitation [16], flotation [17], oxidation [18], electrochemical removal [19–20], reduction [21], ion exchange [22], filtration [23], reverse osmosis [24], and adsorption [3–4]. Water treatment should be affordable, and cost-effective. Among the stated methods, it has been proven that adsorption is one of the most efficient and operative methods with high productivity and significant economic benefits [15].

To achieve higher removal efficiency, it is necessary to significantly increase the specific surface area of the adsorbent as researchers have used various compounds such as bentonite [25], montmorillonite [26], and graphene [13], hydroxides. In recent years, layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have been used as effective adsorbents for toxic heavy metals removal from aqueous media [27–28]. LDHs have received a great deal of attention as a family of two-dimensional layered anionic materials with a general formula
M
1 - x
II
M x
III
OH
2
x +
A
n -
x n
. m
H 2
o
, due to structural flexibility and chemical adaptability, with the ability to uniformly distribute metal cations at the atomic scale in brucite-like layers and the ability to combine a variety of interlayer anions [29–30].
M
II
and
M
III
represent divalent and trivalent metal ions in the LDH sheet that occupy octagonal cavities in a brucite-like layer, and
A
n -
refers to the interlayer anions [27]. In particular, LDHs are widely used as adsorbents due to their layered structure, large surface area, good adsorption capacity, and ion exchange properties [13,28,30].

Carbonaceous materials have been widely used to remove heavy metals due to their highly active functional groups and their ability to interact electrostatically with target ions [31]. In the meantime, the effect of carbon dots has been well demonstrated with functional groups –COOH, –NH2, -SH and –OH to remove heavy metals [32]. These zero-dimensional nanomaterials have high optical performance, good solubility in water and biocompatibility with the environment [33]. However, the use of these nanomaterials in water treatment is challenging due to their high solubility [12,34]. For this purpose, LDH can be used as a substrate with a positive surface charge and carbon dot stabilization [11].

In this study, LDH/carbon dot (C-dot) nanocomposite was synthesized using coprecipitation and hydrothermal methods, respectively. Morphological characteristics and crystalline state were investigated using the required method. Besides, the adsorption capacity of the composite for Pb2+ under optimal conditions was measured by considering the parameters of pH, temperature, adsorption time, stirring speed, adsorbent dose, etc. Also, adsorption isotherms and kinetic of the composite were studied.

All reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and used as received without purification. All solutions were prepared with deionized water (DI) (Milipore, Mili-Q Gradient A10). L-cysteine, magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (
Mg
N
O 3
2
· 6
H 2
O
), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (
Al
N
O 3
3
· 9
H 2
O
), citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (
C 2
H 4
N
H 2
2
), triethylenetetramine (TETA), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (
Fe
N
O 3
3
· 9
H 2
O
), cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (
Cd
N
O 3
2
· 4
H 2
O
), lead nitrate (
Pb
N
O 3
2
), nickel (II) sulfate hexahydrate (
NiSO 4
· 6
H 2
O
), calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (
Ca
N
O 3
2
· 4
H 2
O
) and manganese (II) nitrate tetrahydrate (
Mn
N
O 3
2
· 4
H 2
O
) were purchased from Merck and Sigma-Aldrich.

Synthesis of
Mg - A l - C
O 3
LDH was following the co-precipitation method [31]. 2 mmol
Mg
N
O 3
2
· 6
H 2
O
and 1 mmol
Al
N
O 3
3
· 9
H 2
O
were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. Mixture was pH-adjusted to 10 by alkali solution. The alkali solution containing of NaOH (2 M) and
NaNO 3
(0.2 M) were added drop-wise to the mixed salt solution for 30 min in
N 2
atmosphere under vigorous stirring. The slurry suspension was aged at 80℃ for 20 h. The obtained white precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water three times and dried at 60℃ for 24 h.

The C-dots were prepared using hydrothermal method [11]. Briefly, in the first method, 0.5 g citric acid (CA) and 0.25 or 0.57 mL ethylenediamine (EDA) (CD1 and CD2) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The homogeneous solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 150℃ for 2 h. In the second method, C-dots (CD3) were synthesized based on the method of Dong et al. [33]. In brief, 0.1 g CA and 0.5 g L-cysteine were mixed in 2.5 mL of deionized water. The homogeneous solution was aged at oven with 70℃ for 24 h. The yellow and viscose sediment kept in oil bath at 180-195℃ for 90 min. The resultant dark brown paste was dissolved in de-ionized water and added a few drops of NaOH 0.2 M to neutralize to pH 7.6.

Mg-Al LDH-Carbon dot nanocomposites were prepared based on the method of Zhang et al. [12]. 5 mL carbon dot colloidal solution was mixed with 150 mg Mg-Al-NO3 LDH under vigorous stirring and kept in contact for 60 min. The obtained products were collected by centrifugation and washed with distilled water three times and then dried in the oven to obtain

The capacity of an adsorbent is determined by certain constants, the values of which express the surface properties and affinity of the adsorbent. Batch adsorption experiments were performed to investigate the effect of initial pH value (2–9), contact time (5–150 min), the dosage of Mg-Al LDH-CDs (8–17.6 mg) adsorbent, CD to Mg-Al LDH ratio, and different ionic strength on the Pb2+ adsorption capacity. 0.16 mg of synthesized nanocomposite was added to 40 mL of Pb2+ solution (10 mg.L-1) and stirred for 90 min. The concentration of ions before and after adsorption, were measured by FAAS. The equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) and removal efficiency percentages (Removal %) of metal ions were calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2) [13–14].
(1)
q e
=
C 0
- C
e
V
M
(2)
Removal\%
=
C 0
-
C e
C 0
× 100
where qe (mg.g−1) and R (%) represent the amount of ion adsorbed at equilibrium and removal percentages, respectively. C0 (mg.L-1) and Ci (mg.L-1) represent the initial and equilibrium Pb2+ ions concentrations of the solutions, respectively. M (g) is the mass of adsorbent and V (L) is the volume of the mixed suspension.

Various equations and isotherms have been developed to describe the adsorbent behavior. The parameters obtained from the different models provide important information about the adsorption mechanism, the affinities of adsorbents and the surface properties. The Langmuir, Freundlich, and Dubinin-Radushkevich (D-R) equations were tested to confirm the adsorption behavior of Mg-Al LDH-CDs.

Langmuir, Freundlich and Dubinin-Radushkevich models follow the Eqs. (3)–(5), respectively.
(3)
C e
q e
=
C e
q max
+
1
b
q
max
(4)
Logq e
= LogK
F
+ (
1 n
)LogC e
(5)
ln
q e
= l n
q max
- β
ε 2
where qmax (mg.g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity reflected to a complete monolayer coverage and b is an affinity constant related to the energy of adsorption. The amount of adsorbate concentration
C e
(mg.L-1) decreases as a result of the liquid containing the adsorbent to the concentration of
C 0
(mg.L-1) for sufficient time in contact with the adsorbent. Freundlich constants, KF and n are related to adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity, respectively. The D-R isotherm is based on molecule adsorbed porous structure with a Gaussian energy distribution onto heterogeneous surface [14,35–37]. β (mol2.kJ−2) is a D-R isotherm constant related to the energy of adsorption, and ε (kJ.mol−1) is the adsorption potential. The adsorption potential (ε) was calculated by Eq. (6).
(6)
ε = RT ln
1 +
1
C e
where R (8.314 J.mol-1K−1) is the gas constant, and T is the absolute temperature (K).

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) is used to identify functional groups in CDs. Fig. 1 a-d shows the FT-IR spectra of Mg-Al LDH, Mg-Al LDH-CD1, Mg-Al LDH-CD2 and Mg-Al LDH-CD3 in the range of 400–4000 cm−1, respectively. The broad adsorption bands at 3446 cm−1 and weak band at 1630 cm−1 were assigned to the hydroxyl groups (–OH) and H2O bending and stretching vibration of the interlayer water Mg-Al LDH. High intensity indicates a large number of hydroxyl groups and water molecules in the structure of the synthesized Mg-Al LDH. The strong band at 1360 cm−1 (symmetric stretch vibration mode of nitrate (N-O)) and weak band at 954 cm−1 were assigned to the O-N groups. As a result, the presence of
NO 3 -
anions is confirmed in the interlayer of Mg-Al LDH. The bands observed at 785 and 679 cm−1 could possibly show deformation of Al-OH and Mg-OH, respectively [11,13,31,38].

The XRD patterns of the C-dot, Mg-Al LDH and Mg-Al LDH-CD3 are showed in the Fig. 2 a-c, respectively. The XRD patterned of the C-dot showed a reflection peak at 2θ = 28° attributed to crystallinity of carbon dot (Fig. 2a). All diffraction peaks in Fig. 2b and 2c can be referred to the Mg-Al-NO3 LDH hydrotalcite phase of the standard Mg-Al-NO3 LDH database (JCPDS no. 38–0487). The sharp peaks situated around at 2θ = 11°, 23°, 34°, 39°, 46°, 60° and 62° may be corresponded to the (003), (006), (009), (015), (018), (110) and (113) plans of Mg-Al LDH, respectively. Compared to the XRD pattern of the C-dot (Fig. 2a) and Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite (Fig. 2c) composition, peak at around 2θ = 28° is relatively weak to be observed, which may be due to its relatively low diffraction intensity in the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite [13,31,38].

The morphology of the synthesized Mg-Al-NO3 LDH and Mg-Al LDH/C-dots nanocomposite further characterized by SEM and shown in Fig. 3 . According to the SEM images, the synthesized samples were of uniform size and shape that of some sheets and clarified its layered structure. In the Fig. 3a and b, aggregation and agglomeration were observed, which is acceptable considering the particle size and morphology. The quantitative elemental analyses of the Mg-Al-NO3 LDH and Mg-Al LDH/C-dots nanocomposites obtained by EDS and results were shown in Fig. 4 a and b, respectively. There are Mg, Al, N, O, Na elements in the structure of LDH as shown in The EDS spectra of the Mg-Al-NO3 LDH. Beside the presence of these elements, the addition of carbon and sulfur elements to the EDX spectra in Mg-Al LDH/C-dots nanocomposite confirms the formation of the composite. The normalized fluorescence spectra of the C-dots at different excitation wavelength are showed in Fig. 5 . The excitation wavelength was selected at 350, 300 and 345 nm for CD1, CD2 and CD3, respectively. The optimum emission wavelength for the synthesized C-dots include CD1, CD2 and CD3 was found to be at 455, 470 and 455 nm, respectively.

In order to select the appropriate Pb2+ adsorbent in the initial test were examined three Mg-Al LDH/CD1, Mg-Al LDH/CD2 and Mg-Al LDH/CD3 nanocomposite. The Pb2+ adsorption percentages for nanosorbents were 60, 66.7 and 97%, respectively. According to the results, Mg-Al LDH/CD3 nanocomposite has been selected and then used.

The pH effect was investigated in the 2–9 range. According Fig. 6 a, it has been shown an increase in the removal of Pb2+ by increasing of pH from 2 to 5. It is observed that changing pH in 5–9 has no significant effects on the removal efficiency of Pb2+. Therefore, pH = 7 was selected as the optimal value for the next steps, without the required initial preparation. On the other hand, the reason for the inefficiency of the adsorbent in acidic pH (<4) can be attributed to the change in Mg-Al LDH crystal structure [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-dot to Mg-Al LDH ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of weight ratio of C-dot in Mg-Al LDH adsorbent was investigated on the Pb2+ adsorption. As shown in Fig. 6b, the ion adsorption capacity of the adsorbent increased as the C-dot content increases to 0.1. This increase in removal efficiency of Pb2+ can be attributed to the presence of functional groups of C-dot. Ratio 0.25 was selected as the optimal point; because after adding more C-dot in combination with Mg-Al LDH, it has little effect on adsorption and Pb2+ removal percentage.

The amount of adsorbent has a direct effect on the number of active sites in metal adsorption. Under stable conditions to investigate the effect of adsorbent dosage on Pb2+ removal rate, experiments were performed at 8, 9.6, 11.2, 12.8, 14.4, 16 and 17.6 mg of adsorbent. The results of Pb2+ ion removal by Mg-Al LDH/C-dot are shown in Fig. 6c and show that Pb2+ removal increased with an increase in adsorbent dosage. The highest change in the removal efficiency was 16 mg, so it was considered as the optimum value of the synthesized adsorbent. As the amount of adsorbent increased, the active sites become more available and the removal efficiency increased. This increase continues until all the ions in the sample are adsorbed, after that the removal efficiency will not be affected by further adsorbent.

To investigate the effect of contact time on the adsorption efficiency of Pb2+ ions on Mg-Al LDH/C-dot, the experiment was performed under stable conditions with varying time from 5 to 150 min. Mg-Al LDH/C-dot Pb2+ removal efficiency as a function of contact time was shown in Fig. 6d. In the primary stages, the rate of Pb2+ ions removal by Mg-Al LDH/C-dot can be attributed to the availability of increased number active sites on the adsorbent, which causes to the rapid removal of Pb2+ ions. As time passes and the active sites are occupied, the adsorption capacity reaches an almost constant value, which indicates the involvement of the functional groups and the saturation of the adsorbent [11]. As shown in Fig. 6d, the Pb2+ ion removal efficiency increased with increasing time to 40 min and then remained approximately constant. Therefore, the optimal contact time of 40 min was selected.

Ionic strength was also examined as an influential parameter. Sodium chloride salt was used for this experiment in the concentrations of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1 M. The solution was tested under the optimal conditions obtained previously. Fig. 7 shows the adsorption capacity of adsorbent at different concentrations of sodium chloride salt. By adding a small amount of salt to the solution, the percentage of Pb2+ removal was slightly reduced, but it significantly reduced by further increasing the ionic strength of the solution. This decrease in the adsorption indicates that the increase in ionic strength is a disturbing factor in the adsorption of the target ion. This disturbance can be attributed to the saturation of the solution with ions, which are not removed by the adsorbent. Probably, the interfering ion prevents effective interactions with the Pb2+ ion by creating temporary and unstable interactions with the adsorbent. This has caused a significant decrease in adsorption.

The adsorption process is a competitive phenomenon and there may be different cationic species present in the real sample (mixture metal systems). Therefore, it is necessary to study the adsorption process in the presence of interfering ions. Experiments were performed under stable conditions in the presence of heavy metal ions including Cobalt, Nickel, Copper, Manganese, Zinc, Calcium and Cadmium. The test solution contained 10 ppm of interference ion at 40 mL. For this purpose, the first solution containing metal ions were examined separately. The results are shown in Fig. 8 .

According to Fig. 8a, a comparison was made between Nano adsorbent and LDH. It was observed that Nano adsorbent has a high ability to remove heavy metals compared to LDH. It seems that presence of Carbon dots in adsorbent structure is effective. This may be due to the interaction between Pb2+ and carbon dots special functional groups. This can also be attributed to the almost similar adsorption behavior of the adsorbent for the metal ions. In the second stage, a competitive was investigated solution containing Pb2+ and other metal ions including cadmium, nickel and cobalt. The reason for selecting the three metal ions was the high adsorption rate of the sample by the nano sorbent. As shown in Fig. 8b, in a solution containing four metal ions, the percentage of Pb2+ removal was about 80%, while for other metal ions in the highest amount was about 20%. Since the Mg-Al LDH/C-dots nanocomposite has higher removal efficiency for Pb2+, further studies were performed on removal of this metal ion.

Equilibrium adsorption isotherms are one of the most important data for understanding the adsorption mechanism. Adsorption isotherms express how the adsorbent molecules are distributed between the solid and the liquid phase when the adsorption process is in equilibrium and reveal the constant values of the adsorption isotherms, the surface properties and the adsorbent capacity. In this study, Langmuir, Freundlich and Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherm models were investigated. The diagram and values of the isotherms are shows in Fig. 9 and Table 1 . According to the obtained correlation coefficient (R2) values listed in Table 1, the adsorption process follows the Langmuir isotherm equation with R2 equal to 0.9938. On the other hand, according to the Freundlich isotherm (Eq. (4)), if n = 1, is independent of the concentration of the division between the two phases, and if 1 >
1 / n
is similar to the Langmuir isotherm. If
1 / n
> 1 indicates the dependence of adsorption. Therefore,
1 / n
=
1 /
3.66
= 0.2732
<1, the adsorbent behavior follows the Langmuir isotherm. It could be consummated that the adsorption of Pb2+ is a monolayer adsorption applicable to homogeneous surfaces.

The adsorption kinetics behavior of the proposed sorbent was studied using three well-known kinetics models, i.e. pseudo-first-order (rate constant of pollutant adsorption), pseudo-second-order model (based on equilibrium adsorption), and intra particle diffusion [14,35–36]. The models were given by the following Eqs. (7), (8) and (9), respectively.
(7)
log
q e
-
q t
= log
q e
-
k 1
t
2.303
(8)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q e 2
+
t
q e
(9)
q t
=
k
id
t
1 / 2
+ c
where qe and qt are the amounts of adsorbate adsorbed by a certain amount of adsorbent at equilibrium (mg.g−1) and at time t (S). k1, k2 and kid are the pseudo-first-order rate constant (min−1), pseudo-second-order rate constant (g.mg-1min−1) and intra-particle diffusion rate constant (mg.g-1min−0.5), respectively. The intra-particle diffusion constant (c) is proportional to the boundary layer thickness. Fig. 10 a and 10b shows the pseudo-first-order model, pseudo-second-order model, and intra-particle diffusion, respectively. The acquired parameters values are also shown in Table 2 . The experimental qe (mg.g−1) obtained for Mg-Al LDH/C-dot could be well fitted by pseudo-second-order model, and closer values to the experimental values (Table 2). Hence, the interactions between the adsorbent and Pb2+ ions are of the chemical bonds type and act more strongly than Van der Waals bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of Pb2+ ion adsorption capacities with different adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: Maximum adsorption capacity (qm) of other adsorbents for the removal of Pb2+ ion from aqueous phase are shown in Table 3 . According to the results, the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite has an acceptable performance compared to other adsorbents, which can be attributed to the presence of several functional groups in the structure of the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite. It should be noted that due to the differences in the components used in the structure of nanocomposites and differences in laboratory conditions, the comparison of adsorbent adsorption capacity in general does not accurately indicate the ability or weakness of adsorbents. For this purpose, it is necessary to consider such things as simplicity of synthesis method, environmentally friendly, accessibility, economically, reusability, industrialization capability, etc.

The Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite reusability parameter was investigated by three cycles of the adsorption and desorption process. Na2EDTA solution was used to desorb Pb2+ from adsorbent in pH = 10. The results showed that the desorption of Pb2+ ion was performed well. The new adsorbent to be reusable up to three cycles without decreasing the ability. The adsorption efficiencies were obtained for the three cycles: 93.5%, 93% and 93%, respectively. As it turns out, after three consecutive testing periods, the Pb2+ removal rate was at a stable level. This case showed that Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite was practical applicability and can be used for real samples.

The Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposites were successfully synthesized by coprecipitation and hydrothermal methods. The chemical structure and morphology of Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposites were investigated by FTIR, XRD, SEM, EDS and FS. According to the results, the addition of carbon dots to the Mg-Al LDH was led to a significant increase in the strength and ability the adsorption of heavy metals such as Pb2+, which could be attributed to the presence of different functional groups on the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite. The Pb2+ adsorption isotherm on the surface of the Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposite was proportional to the Langmuir model, indicative of a monolayer adsorption. The adsorption kinetics followed the pseudo-second-order that confirmed the chemical adsorption. The reusability and recovery of the adsorbent indicated its practical applicability. The results show that Mg-Al LDH/C-dot nanocomposites can be considered as a promising adsorbent for the management of environmental pollution of heavy metals, especially Pb2+, in the future.

Fataneh Vasheghani Farahani: Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Mohammad Hassan Amini: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Writing - original draft, Supervision, Project administration. Seyed Hamid Ahmadi: Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Writing - original draft, Supervision. Seyed Amirabbas Zakaria: Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran is gratefully acknowledged.
